content.text: JSX is mostly static markup, but curly braces

open a portal back to JavaScript. Anything inside them is evaluated as a JavaScript expression and its result is embedded into the output. This one mechanism handles variables, computed values, function calls, array transformations, and conditional logic.

attrs.alt: Annotated JSX component showing where curly braces are used for child expressions, prop values, and method calls

content.text: Curly braces can appear in exactly two positions in JSX.
— no quotes. Adding quotes would make it a literal string:
An expression is anything that evaluates to a value. All of the following are valid inside
What Cannot Go Inside Curly Braces
, variable declarations — do not produce a value and cannot be used inside
Move statements before the return and put only their result in
Because only expressions are allowed, React code uses three expression-based patterns for conditional rendering.
Ternary works well when you need to choose between two values or two JSX elements.
When the left side is truthy,
evaluates and returns the right side. When falsy, it short-circuits and returns the left side — which React renders as nothing if it's
if the left side evaluates to
, React renders the number 0 — not nothing. Always use a proper boolean:
JSX children can be an array.
returns an array of JSX elements, so it slots naturally inside
prop — a stable unique string or number within the list. React uses it to reconcile list items efficiently during re-renders.
Any expression can be used as an attribute value. This includes computed class names, dynamic URLs, event handlers, and object values for the
Text and expressions can be freely mixed between JSX tags. React concatenates them:
The double-curly pattern you'll see in inline styles is not special syntax — it's an object literal inside an expression context:
Occasionally you need more than one step of logic inline. An immediately-invoked function expression (IIFE) keeps it in expression form, though extracting to a helper function is usually cleaner:
What JSX Renders for Different Values
Not all values render the same way. Understanding this prevents silent layout bugs:
— objects are not valid React children
Here is a realistic component that uses expressions for computed values, conditional classes, and list rendering all at once:
Extracting Complex Logic into Helper Functions
When an expression inside JSX grows beyond one or two operations, extracting it to a helper keeps the JSX readable:
A useful pattern is computing all derived values in the function body before the
statement. The JSX then becomes nearly declarative — a clean layout with no logic noise:
Because JSX is just a function call, any expression can return it. This enables render helpers — small functions that return JSX without being full components (no capitalization, no props API):
— variable, call, ternary, map — into JSX children or attribute values.
